Yes, the atmosphere shifts entirely when the first few drinks take hold, and that initial "handsome" charm begins to curdle into something far more grating. What started as confident presence quickly descends into a loud, obnoxious spectacle that most people in the room are actively trying to ignore. His speech loses all its poise, devolving into a series of slurred, repetitive rants and forced, theatrical mannerisms that feel desperate rather than endearing.
​As he becomes more intoxicated, the "vibrant" persona he thinks he's projecting comes across as invasive and messy. He clings to the center of attention not through charisma, but through sheer volume and a complete lack of social awareness. The aesthetic appeal that usually works in his favor is overshadowed by bloodshot eyes and an aggressive, stumbling energy that makes the people around him exchange uncomfortable glances. Instead of being the life of the party, he becomes the person everyone quietly maneuvers away from, leaving a trail of spilled drinks and exhausted patience in his wake.

When the Greeks adopted the Phoenician alphabet around the 8th century BC, they ran into a problem: their language didn't really have that harsh guttural sound. However, different regions handled the letter \text{H} in two distinct ways:
​The "H" Sound (Heta): In Western Greek dialects (and eventually the Latin alphabet), the letter kept its consonantal duty, representing the h sound we use in "house."
​The Vowel Shift (Eta): In Eastern dialects, specifically Ionic, the h sound had largely disappeared from speech. Instead of discarding the letter, they repurposed it to represent a long, open "e" sound (like the a in "care").
​Victory of the Vowel
​In 403 BC, Athens officially adopted the Ionic alphabet. This was a turning point. From that moment on in the Greek world, \text{H} was no longer a breathy consonant; it was officially the vowel Eta.
​Because it represented a long vowel, it became essential for distinguishing meanings in written Greek. While Epsilon (\epsilon) handled the short "eh" sound, Eta (\eta) took on the heavy lifting for the long "ay" or "ee" sounds.
